rulings.Case Title: ABDELKADER ELGAWADY VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For A Protective Order Preventing Plaintiff From Taking The Deposition Of Department Of Public Works Director Mohammed Nuru
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, March 8, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion For A Protective Order Preventing Plaintiff From Taking The Deposition Of Department Of Public Works Director Mohammed Nuru. Pro Tem Judge Charles Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted. The Court finds that Mohammed Nuru is Director of the Department of Public Works, and is considered a high ranking government official. Under Westly v Sup. Ct. (2004) 125 Cal.App.4th 907 and Nagle v. Sup. Ct. (1994) 28 Cal.App.4th 1465, Mr. Nuru should not be the first witness deposed, as there are undoubtedly lower level witnesses the City could designate to testify about the circumstances of this trip and fall accident. The plaintiff may obtain discovery into this trip and fall accident by deposing the City's person most knowledgeable on the categories designated by plaintiff pursuant to C.C.P. § 2025.230. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to cgeerhart@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ALI R. SHAKOORI VS. SHABAN A SHAKOORI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiff Ali Shakoori
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, March 8, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANTS SHABAN SHAKOORI, JULIA SHAKOORI's Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiff Ali Shakoori Pro Tem Judge Charles Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. The Court finds that the parties had agreed to February 22 as the deposition date for Mr. Shakoori. The notice served by mail by defendants' counsel on February 6 appears to have been properly and timely served. Why it was not received is a mystery, but under Evid. Code sec. 641, "A letter correctly addressed and properly mailed is presumed to have been received in the ordinary course of mail." The amended notice, which was electronically served on February 18, was not timely, but the deposition still should have gone forward as originally noticed on the agreed upon date. The Court orders that the deponent Ali Shakoori shall appear for deposition within one week of this order, and shall produce at deposition the documents identified in the attachment (Exh. A) to the original deposition notice served February 6. Because the Court cannot determine whether the original deposition notice was in fact delivered (and plaintiff's counsel and his staff deny that it was), sanctions are denied as to both sides. The parties should have worked together to make the deposition happen without court intervention. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to cgeerhart@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. rulings.Case Title: RAMONA SCOTT ET AL VS. LEGION CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion, Motion And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents And Things, Set Two And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, March 8, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFFS RAMONA SCOTT, RAMON SCOTT'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Production Of Documents And Things, Set Two And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Charles Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted. Defendant Legion shall serve a complete amended response, without objections, within 30 days of service of this order. (The Court does find that Legion's response to demand for production, served by mail January 22, was timely served.) Under Pioneer Electronics v. Sup. Ct. (2007) 40 Cal.4th 360, 373, it is settled law that plaintiffs are entitled to discover the identities and contact information of other potential class members. However, because employee records are sought, there is a privacy issue, subject to the Court's balancing the parties' discovery needs vs. privacy. Legion was thus not without substantial justification in refusing to produce the records without a court order. Sanctions denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to cgeerhart@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
